stories.title: Online tool connects farmers with available land to cattle ranchers in need of grazing area after wildfires
stories.summary: After Nebraska wildfires burned 800,000+ acres, ranchers faced a critical shortage of grazing land for their cattle. The Nebraska Grazing Exchange created a free online platform connecting livestock producers with farmers offering available pasture, sparking nationwide generosity as neighbors help neighbors move herds to safety across state lines.
stories.extended_summary: Some ranchers lost 70-80% of their summer grazing range in a matter of days. The Nebraska Grazing Exchange launched as a matchmaking service: farmers with open land create profiles, desperate ranchers send messages, and then comes the logistical puzzle of moving entire herds—sometimes to different states entirely. Truckloads of donated hay are flowing in, but grazing land offers a sustainable solution. Laura Field of the Nebraska Cattlemen's Association says help is arriving from "all directions, North, South, East, and West." What could have been an economic catastrophe is becoming a testament to agricultural community bonds—strangers opening their gates to save each other's livelihoods.

stories.title: Amid energy market turmoil, the people taking power into their own hands
stories.summary: East London entrepreneur Reg Platt is transforming council housing estates into neighborhood power stations. His company installs solar panels on flat roofs, creating 'micro grids' that supply clean electricity directly to tenants at lower rates while selling surplus power back to the national grid—making renewable energy local, affordable, and community-owned.
stories.extended_summary: Picture a rock star-looking entrepreneur named Reg Platt—yes, really—wiring up sprawling council flat rooftops across Hackney with solar panels. His company, Emergent Energy, exploits new regulations allowing locally generated power to flow directly to nearby households instead of through distant utilities. Tenants get billed for the electricity they use at competitive rates, and when local demand drops, surplus power gets exported to the National Grid for revenue. It's a model that turns social housing into a distributed clean energy network, proving renewable power doesn't have to come from remote wind farms or corporate solar fields. It can be hyperlocal, affordable, and owned by the people who actually use it.
stories.headline_split: Amid energy market turmoil, the people taking power

stories.title: Route-planning AI cut climate-warming contrails on over 100 flights
stories.summary: Google researchers tested AI-planned flight routes on 2,400+ real commercial flights between the US and Europe, successfully reducing contrails—the condensation streaks that cause more warming than aircraft CO2 emissions. The AI predicted ice-rich atmospheric regions and rerouted planes around them, proving the technology works at commercial scale for the first time.
stories.extended_summary: Those white streaks trailing behind jets? They trap more heat than the carbon dioxide planes emit. Google's AI learned to predict which patches of sky would spawn contrails using detailed weather forecasts, then recommended alternate routes to American Airlines pilots during 17 weeks of eastbound transatlantic flights. Over 2,400 flights participated in the randomized trial from January to May 2025, and the system worked—planes following AI guidance produced measurably fewer contrails. Earlier small-scale tests hinted this was possible, but this marks the first time contrail-reduction tech has been deployed across real commercial aviation at scale, not just in controlled experiments.

stories.title: California Legislature Solved One Piece of Homelessness, Now They Need to Renew the Program
stories.summary: California's Pet Assistance and Support program solved a stubborn barrier keeping people on the streets: shelter policies forcing them to choose between housing and their pets. By funding kennels, vet care, and pet-friendly staffing at 61 sites, the program helped 20% of participants—886 people—move into permanent housing. Now the funding is set to expire.
stories.extended_summary: Nearly half of unhoused pet owners had been turned away from shelters or refused services because animals weren't allowed. Many chose the street over surrendering their companion. California's solution was elegantly simple: give shelters $15.75 million to accommodate pets through kennels, veterinary care, food, and dedicated staff. Over four years, 4,407 people were served, and 886 moved into permanent housing—a 20% success rate. Service providers reported reaching individuals who had previously refused all help. The program proved that removing one barrier could unlock housing stability for hundreds. Yet despite measurably working, it now faces an expiration date, threatening to slam the door shut again on pet owners experiencing homelessness.
stories.headline_split: California Legislature Solved One Piece of Homelessness, Now They Need to
fake_endings: convince other states to adopt the successful model
secure federal matching funds before the deadline
stories.fake_headline: Denver Barber Offers Free Haircuts and Job Interview Coaching, Helps 400 People Find Employment

stories.title: Beyond the Classroom: How School Districts Are Building Real-World Career Pathways
stories.summary: When a Denver-area water plant discovered an algae problem, it didn't call engineers—it called high school students. The aquatic robotics team at Colorado's Innovation Center sent underwater robots into the pipes, identified the species, helped solve the problem, and now gets hired for quarterly checkups. This is career education that's actually real work.
stories.extended_summary: Students at St. Vrain Valley Schools' Innovation Center aren't practicing for careers—they're already doing them. Their aquatic robotics team contracts with a water-treatment facility for quarterly pipe inspections. Neighboring towns are calling too. This reflects a broader shift: districts across the U.S. are building career pathways that connect students directly with professional challenges, industry mentors, and paychecks. Research shows career and technical education significantly boosts academic achievement, high school completion, and employability skills. The Education Commission of the States now prioritizes building these pathways through 2027. The question districts face isn't whether to offer career learning anymore—it's whether those pathways lead somewhere real. In Colorado, the answer is a student team solving actual infrastructure problems for actual clients.
stories.headline_split: Beyond the Classroom: How School Districts Are Building Real-World

stories.title: Dozens in Haiti rely on savings groups to access credit where bank loans are unavailable
stories.summary: In Port-au-Prince, community savings groups called AVÈK provide small loans through collective savings, offering an alternative to banks for vulnerable Haitians. One member used a $3,846 loan to buy a delivery truck, generating daily income and eventually funding a neighborhood vocational school—though sustainability challenges remain amid Haiti's instability.
stories.extended_summary: Julmice Bastien borrowed 500,000 gourdes (about $3,846) from his AVÈK savings group and bought a box truck. He contracted it to delivery drivers, splitting the daily $23 revenue and eventually earning enough to operate a neighborhood vocational school. AVÈK groups were introduced after Haiti's 2010 earthquake to boost economic resilience where bank loans are unavailable. Members pool savings and grant small loans to each other for businesses and income-generating activities. The model has supported financial inclusion and entrepreneurship across Haiti, particularly for vulnerable populations locked out of traditional banking. But sustainability remains fragile: repayment risks, lack of loan guarantees, and Haiti's ongoing economic collapse and gang violence all threaten the system. Still, for members like Bastien, it's been a path to financial independence in a country with few alternatives.

stories.title: Farm turns to old-school conservation, new crop to weather drought concerns
stories.summary: As Utah faces its worst drought in memory, a seventh-generation family farm in Spanish Fork is surviving through decades-old conservation methods—concrete-lined ditches, laser-leveled fields, runoff recycling—and a bold new crop: 250,000 tulips. The colorful flowers will keep the farm profitable even as other fields sit idle.
stories.extended_summary: Rex Larsen has never seen it this dry. The Spanish Fork River is running at 50% normal, snowpack is at historic lows, and some of his fields will have to remain unplanted. But Larsen Family Farms isn't just hoping for rain—it's been preparing for this for years. Every ditch is lined with concrete to prevent water loss. Fields are laser-leveled for maximum irrigation efficiency. Runoff gets captured and reused. These aren't flashy tech solutions; they're old-school conservation methods passed down through seven generations of farmers. Now Larsen's daughter Kara has added a new strategy: 250,000 tulips planted last December. The drought-tolerant crop will bloom in vibrant color and generate income even as wheat and alfalfa fields sit idle, proving that legacy farms can adapt and survive even the driest years on record.
stories.headline_split: Farm turns to old-school conservation, new crop to weather

competition from corporate agricultural operations nearby

stories.title: Monthly Cash Payments Could Boost Disaster Survivors. A Maui Nonprofit Tries It Out
stories.summary: After the Lahaina fires destroyed her life and health crisis forced her to quit work, Mari Younger was starving in a converted hotel room, down to 89 pounds. A new cash assistance program started sending her $700 monthly on a Mastercard—and it's been transformative. She's one of 600+ Maui fire survivors now receiving flexible, dignified support through direct payments.
stories.extended_summary: Mari Younger was down to 89 pounds, living in a converted hotel room 30 miles from the Lahaina life she'd loved for 11 years. Her disability payments couldn't cover health insurance, car payments, and the food she desperately needed to survive. She told herself she'd "rather starve" than let her cat Stella go hungry. Then a case manager told her about a new cash assistance program for Maui fire survivors. Now $700 arrives monthly on a Mastercard, giving Younger agency over her own recovery. "It's like the cavalry has shown up," she said. She's among 600+ fire survivors receiving direct cash payments—a model proponents say offers dignity and flexibility traditional disaster aid can't match. People know what they need better than any agency does. For Younger, it meant food, stability, and hope that the war isn't over but help is finally coming.
stories.headline_split: Monthly Cash Payments Could Boost Disaster Survivors. A Maui Nonprofit

stories.title: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation around alcohol
stories.summary: Danay Jones founded Sober Girls Houston after realizing she needed community, not just therapy, to sustain sobriety. Her nonprofit now hosts alcohol-free concerts, restaurant meetups, and DJ nights across Houston for women in recovery and the "sober curious"—millennials and Gen Z rethinking their relationship with drinking without labeling themselves as addicts.
stories.extended_summary: Danay Jones hit her breaking point during COVID: legal issues, mental health struggles, physical problems—all tied to alcohol that had once been social currency through constant moves and new cities. She quit, started therapy, found a psychiatrist, took up yoga. But what she really needed was community. So in 2022, she built it. Sober Girls Houston hosts events across the city—concerts, restaurants, live DJs—with one rule: you come sober. It's for women in recovery, yes, but also for the "sober curious," a growing wave of millennials and Gen Z choosing to be more intentional about when, why, and how much they drink. They're not quitting forever or labeling themselves addicts. They're just pausing, getting curious, choosing presence. In a city famous for Sunday Funday brunches and rooftop happy hours, a shift is happening—one alcohol-free DJ night at a time.
stories.headline_split: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation

stories.title: Wheat on Repeat
stories.summary: After 20 years of research, Kansas scientists created Kernza—a perennial wheat-like grain that produces multiple harvests from one plant without replanting. The crop slashes emissions, stores carbon in soil, and reduces fertilizer and machinery use. Its flavor? Surprisingly warm, with hints of cinnamon and nutmeg.
stories.extended_summary: Lee DeHaan tells you not to drown Kernza pasta in sauce—you need to taste it. And when you do, there's cinnamon, then nutmeg, an earthy warmth tied to the Kansas soil it came from. But the real breakthrough isn't the flavor. It's that this grain never needs replanting. Unlike wheat, rice, and corn (which feed half of humanity and require annual planting, fertilizers, herbicides, and gas-guzzling machinery), Kernza is perennial. One plant, multiple harvests, year after year. That means slashed emissions, carbon stored in deep root systems, and healthier soil. The Land Institute spent two decades developing it. Now it's commercially available, a tantalizing glimpse of climate-friendly agriculture that doesn't require farmers to start over every spring. The fusilli in front of you isn't just pasta—it's proof that a better way to grow food is possible.
stories.headline_split: Wheat on

stories.title: How 1700 Blind Travellers Explored 16 Destinations in India & Abroad
stories.summary: Amit Jain's Rising Star Khilte Chehre has enabled 1,700 blind travelers to explore 16 destinations across India and abroad. The program blends accessibility, affordability, and empathy to restore independence and confidence for visually impaired Indians who thought their traveling days were over.
stories.extended_summary: Dr. Hitesh Prasad stopped traveling after losing his vision in his late twenties — friends were supportive but couldn't always provide the cues he needed to navigate crowds or landscapes. Then he discovered Rising Star Khilte Chehre (RSKC), Amit Jain's inclusive travel initiative that has now taken 1,700 blind travelers to 16 destinations from Delhi to Thailand. The program doesn't just make travel possible — it restores the independence and confidence that vision loss had taken away, turning what seemed like a closed chapter into a nationwide movement.

stories.title: Help for Hemlocks
stories.summary: A tiny beetle introduced to Harriman State Park is thriving and spreading, offering hope for New York's hemlock trees devastated by an invasive pest. Between 2018-2020, researchers released 2,000 Laricobius nigrinus beetles that eat the tree-killing adelgids — now the population has exploded and spread 30 miles, allowing relocation efforts across the state.
stories.extended_summary: For decades, New York's hemlocks have been dying, ravaged by the fuzzy hemlock woolly adelgid. But the sesame-seed-sized cavalry has arrived: Laricobius nigrinus, a beetle that eats only adelgids and nothing else. Cornell researchers and volunteers released about 2,000 of these beetles in Harriman State Park between 2018 and 2020, hoping they'd establish a foothold. Recent checks revealed the population didn't just survive — it exploded, with beetles now found almost 30 miles from the original release sites. The boom is allowing researchers to relocate beetles to the Catskills, Finger Lakes, and Lake George.

stories.title: I paused my PhD for 11 years to help save Madagascar’s seas
stories.summary: Ando Rabearisoa paused her PhD for 11 years to return to Madagascar and create community-managed marine conservation areas. Working with local fishing communities, she helped grow the number of locally managed marine areas from 33 to 177, including one where fish biomass increased 189% in six years.
stories.extended_summary: Three years into a PhD in ecological economics in France, Ando Rabearisoa made a choice that would reshape Madagascar's coastline. In 2009, she abandoned her studies to move home and work with fishing communities to create locally managed marine areas — conservation zones overseen by the people who depend on them, not distant government agencies. As marine programme leader at Conservation International from 2009 to 2019, Rabearisoa helped the number of these areas swell from 33 to 177. In Madagascar's first LMMA, researchers observed a 189% increase in fish biomass over six years, proving that local control can protect both ecosystems and livelihoods.

stories.title: Call a boomer or zoomer: Magic phone booth bridges the generational divide
stories.summary: A bright yellow payphone outside a Boston University coffeehouse connects Gen-Z students with baby boomers in a Nevada senior center. When 21-year-old Ryan Marcantonio picked up the "call a boomer" phone, he was connected to 73-year-old Maria Jaynes, who answered from a matching "call a zoomer" booth at her senior living community.
stories.extended_summary: Ryan Marcantonio nearly stopped in his tracks when he spotted a bright yellow payphone outside a Boston University coffeehouse — and he's someone who tracks payphones. But this one reads "call a boomer" and does exactly that. When the 21-year-old economics major picked up, 73-year-old Maria Jaynes answered from Reno, Nevada, standing at a matching booth that says "call a zoomer." Matter Neuroscience installed the phones as a social experiment to bridge the generational divide. The two strangers compared weather, discussed activities at Jaynes' senior center, and had the kind of unhurried conversation that rarely happens between generations anymore.
stories.headline_split: Call a boomer or zoomer: Magic phone booth

stories.title: Restoration of plaque serves as reminder of history
stories.summary: Gary Estep travels North Texas restoring historical markers for free, inspired by a TikTok video he saw while scrolling. He's restored around 20 markers in two months, saving communities up to $2,500 per replacement while keeping local history visible and honored.
stories.extended_summary: Gary Estep was doom-scrolling TikTok when he came across videos of someone restoring historical markers across Texas. Two months later, he's restored around 20 markers throughout North Texas — for free. When he asked a local newspaper for permission to restore the plaque on their office explaining the paper's history, he showed up the same day and brought the faded gray background back to deep black, the silver lettering back to a shine. Estep learned the process from a Texas Historical Committee how-to video and now volunteers his time to save communities up to $2,500 per marker replacement, keeping local history alive one plaque at a time.

stories.title: These Young Classical Musicians Are Living in a Seattle Retirement Facility
stories.summary: The renowned Balourdet Quartet now lives at Skyline, a Seattle retirement facility, as the Seattle Chamber Music Society's first-ever quartet in residence. Residents get access to performances, open rehearsals, and master classes — plus hallway conversations and dinner invitations with world-class musicians in their 20s and 30s.
stories.extended_summary: A retirement community might not seem like the natural home for a string quartet of young millennials, but Skyline in Seattle has become the perfect landing pad for the renowned Balourdet Quartet. When the Seattle Chamber Music Society needed housing for its first-ever quartet in residence last fall, the senior living facility stepped up. Now residents have access to performances, open rehearsals, lectures, and master classes a couple times a month — plus the chance to invite the musicians to dinner or chat in the hallways. One resident was particularly pleased to preview the same performance the quartet later gave at Carnegie Hall.

stories.title: Cancer Is Not Going To Get In Christopher Hack’s Way
stories.summary: Christopher Hack opened Vacancy in Ann Arbor to solve two problems: talented STAMPS School graduates leaving town because they can't make a living, and artists losing access to specialized equipment after graduation. A year later, not even a cancer diagnosis is slowing down his mission to keep young artists creating in Ann Arbor.
stories.extended_summary: Christopher Hack was tired of watching extremely talented artists graduate from STAMPS School and immediately leave Ann Arbor because the market was too small to make a living. So he opened Vacancy at 221 North Main Street about a year ago to create a free marketplace for artists and provide access to expensive, specialized equipment that many couldn't afford after graduation. The space lets young artists stay in town and keep working in their medium, no matter how "weird" their art is. Now, even a cancer diagnosis isn't stopping Hack's mission to build an ecosystem where contemporary fine art is accessible and affordable to average people.

stories.title: Prevention works — invest in conflict prevention with wolves | FEEDBACK
stories.summary: After 13 years as a range rider in Washington state, the founder of Project GRIPH has proven that proactive wolf management prevents conflict better than compensation after losses. Consistent human presence, livestock monitoring, carcass removal, and prompt hazing have eliminated wolf depredation on participating ranches, conditioning wolves away from livestock.
stories.extended_summary: Colorado's wolf depredation payouts have exceeded $700,000, highlighting a fundamental flaw in how we handle wolf recovery: we compensate after conflict instead of preventing it. As a range rider and founder of Project GRIPH, the author has spent 13 years proving there's a better way. Through consistent human presence on the landscape, livestock monitoring, carcass removal, and prompt hazing when wolves are nearby, participating Washington state ranches have eliminated wolf depredation entirely. The tools require a specific mindset and hands-on work, but they condition wolves away from preying on livestock — allowing ranchers and wolves to move past mere coexistence toward something better.

stories.title: 6,000 Tons of Sediment Kept Out of Streams as South Fork Project Wraps Up
stories.summary: Clinton County's decade-long South Fork Wildcat Creek cleanup has kept over 6,000 tons of sediment out of local streams through 51 conservation practices on 5,000 acres. Two local streams have been removed from Indiana's impaired waters list, and healthier ditches are delaying costly maintenance for landowners.
stories.extended_summary: Clinton County's 10-year effort to clean up the South Fork of Wildcat Creek is wrapping up with the kind of numbers that make the work real: over 6,000 tons of sediment kept out of streams, 9,000 pounds of phosphorus and 30,000 pounds of nitrogen prevented from entering waterways. Resource conservation specialist Michelle Gilbert says 27 landowners participated in more than 51 conservation practices, including nearly 5,000 acres of cover crops and equipment modifications. Two local streams — Prairie Creek and Jenkins Ditch — have been removed from Indiana's impaired waters list. Healthier ditches also mean delayed "dip outs," saving money for landowners and the county surveyor's office.

stories.title: A Teacher Shortage Solution: Grow Your Own
stories.summary: Facing Mississippi's second-highest teacher shortage, Clarksdale is identifying potential teachers before they graduate high school through nonprofit RISE. The approach addresses both staffing challenges and diversity gaps — nearly half of Mississippi students are Black, but only about a quarter of teachers are.
stories.extended_summary: Clarksdale had 40 teacher vacancies posted last July — the second highest shortage in Mississippi. Students were being taught by substitutes for weeks at a time, and administrators were scrambling each late summer to fill positions. So district leaders are trying something researchers recommend: identifying potential teachers early, before they even graduate high school. Adrienne Hudson, who runs Clarksdale nonprofit RISE, says the traditional factory model of teacher production isn't working — supply and demand don't match. The approach also addresses a stubborn diversity gap: nearly half of Mississippi students are Black, but only about a quarter of their teachers are, a gap that's barely budged in 10 years.

stories.title: How a tiny Texas town is using wind energy to help out senior citizens
stories.summary: A small Texas town is using revenue from wind turbines to subsidize electric bills for senior citizens at their community center. The local government partnered with energy companies to direct some of the income from renewable energy directly back to help older residents afford their utilities.
stories.extended_summary: In a small Texas town, the whir of wind turbines is doing more than generating clean power — it's cutting electric bills for seniors at the local community center. The town struck a deal with wind energy companies to funnel a portion of turbine revenue into subsidies that help older residents like 75-year-old Cynthia Flores, who spends afternoons doing puzzles with friends, afford their utilities. It's a rare example of renewable energy profits staying local and supporting the people who live in the shadow of the infrastructure.

stories.title: Scout Troop 214 helps FWP create habitats for perch
stories.summary: Scout Troop 214 collects hundreds of curbside Christmas trees each January and helps Montana Fish, Wildlife, and Parks sink them into Canyon Ferry to create breeding habitat for perch. What used to take FWP a week now takes half a day thanks to the scouts' help threading cables through stumps and attaching cinder blocks.
stories.extended_summary: Every January, Scout Troop 214 fans out across town collecting discarded Christmas trees — not for recycling, but to give fish a home. The scouts drill holes in stumps, thread cables through, add cinder blocks, and create weighted lines that will be dropped by helicopter into Canyon Ferry in April. These underwater evergreen forests become spawning habitat for perch. What Montana Fish, Wildlife, and Parks used to take a week to prepare now takes half a day, and the scouts learn what it means to turn holiday waste into something living.

stories.title: Hopeworks Kensington offers paid job training and career help for young adults
stories.summary: Hopeworks Kensington offers paid training in coding, web design, and job skills for young adults ages 17-26 who are out of work. Participants earn a stipend while learning, then move into $15-16/hour jobs, with many landing full-time positions averaging $43K a year.
stories.extended_summary: Tamaya Temple studied criminal justice and worked in sales, but it wasn't until a friend told her about Hopeworks that she saw a path into tech. The Kensington nonprofit trains young adults ages 17-26 in coding, web design, and public speaking — and pays them while they learn. After passing a readiness test, participants move into real jobs earning $15-16 an hour, with many eventually landing full-time roles averaging $43,000 a year. For young people facing barriers to employment, it's a bridge from uncertainty to a career they once thought was out of reach.

stories.title: First Nation aims to grow its food security in northern ‘B.C.’ — with geothermal heat
stories.summary: West Moberly First Nations in northeastern B.C. plans to use geothermal heat from underground hot water to power greenhouses year-round. The project would improve food security in a region where produce often arrives moldy and traditional foods have become scarce due to environmental disruption.
stories.extended_summary: Moldy strawberries, wilted lettuce, cauliflower gone brown before it hits the shelf — that's what passes for fresh produce in West Moberly First Nations territory, three-and-a-half hours northeast of Prince George, B.C. Decades of dams, seismic lines, forestry, and coal mines have also made traditional foods scarce or unsafe. Now the nation is looking deep underground, where scalding hot, salty water could power year-round greenhouses. If it works, it would be Canada's first geothermal-heated food security project, turning a buried resource into fresh tomatoes, lettuce, and self-reliance.

stories.title: A farmer breeds seeds to withstand Vermont’s unpredictable weather
stories.summary: Charlie Watt founded Homecoming Seeds, a Vermont seed company offering plant varieties adapted to short growing seasons and unpredictable weather. His varieties can germinate in cold soil, resist disease, and tolerate temperature swings — some cultivated by mentors for 40 years.
stories.extended_summary: In Charlie Watt's backyard last August, 20-pound watermelons sprawled next to tomatoes the size of two fists and an eight-foot wall of peas — crops most people wouldn't bet on in Vermont. Watt's new company, Homecoming Seeds, offers varieties adapted to the state's short, unpredictable growing season: plants that can germinate in cold soil, shrug off fungal disease, and still produce abundantly. Some of the seeds come from mentors who've been cultivating them for 40 years. It's patient, unglamorous work — but for Vermont gardeners tired of losing plants to frost, it's a quiet revolution.

stories.extended_summary: Five-year-old Josephine Ziniti sat at her teacher's feet, hand held high, eager to answer a math question — in French. At Manchester's Weston Elementary, kindergartners like Josephine spend all but 30 minutes of their school day learning math, science, reading, and spelling in a second language. The district's dual immersion program, the only one of its kind in New Hampshire, enrolls nearly 80 students in Spanish and French classes. While most schools wait until middle or high school to introduce foreign languages, Manchester is betting that starting early — when brains are wired for language — will give kids a lasting advantage.

stories.summary: Brothers Desmond and Ethan Hua run HOPE, a nonprofit that collects and redistributes gently used school uniforms to low-income families in San Mateo. They've helped 1,400 families and saved them an estimated $140K while keeping textiles out of landfills.
stories.extended_summary: It started with a boy wearing shorts on a cold day. When Desmond and Ethan Hua asked why, he said he didn't have another pair of pants until laundry day. The brothers, students at Bayside Academy in San Mateo, launched HOPE — Help Our Mother Earth — to collect gently used school uniforms and redistribute them to families who can't afford replacements. In their family garage, plastic chests are stacked with neatly folded uniforms for all sizes. So far, they've helped 1,400 families, saving them an estimated $140,000, while keeping thousands of garments out of landfills.

stories.title: Company that raised minimum salaries to $70,000 is still thriving
stories.summary: Nearly seven years after raising all employee salaries to $70K, Gravity Payments has doubled its workforce, reduced turnover, and grown in value. CEO Dan Price cut his own salary to $70K and sold his second home to fund the raises.
stories.extended_summary: When Dan Price raised every Gravity Payments employee's salary to $70,000 in 2015, critics predicted bankruptcy. Nearly seven years later, the credit card processing company has doubled its workforce, slashed turnover, and grown in value — all while maintaining that $70K minimum. Price cut his own salary from over $1 million to $70,000 and sold his second home to make it happen. The company's success flies in the face of conventional wisdom that says CEOs must earn 351 times the average worker to keep a business profitable. Turns out, loyalty and experience matter more than anyone expected.

stories.extended_summary: Jump ropes slammed against the gym floor at Ohio's Circleville Juvenile Correctional Facility as a dozen older teens dropped into pushups in near-unison. They were doing CrossFit — the same grueling high-intensity workout you'd find at any outside gym, but with a different goal. The Expanding Horizons program brings trained coaches into Ohio's three juvenile facilities to work with around 500 incarcerated young people, offering not just physical training but mental support. For youth facing the challenge of reintegration, the program is a chance to build discipline, confidence, and a sense of possibility beyond the walls.

stories.title: This TikToker is trying to revive the nearly extinct Judeo-Arabic language
stories.summary: Dan Sheena, 31, is using TikTok skits and Zoom classes to revive Judeo-Arabic, a nearly extinct language rarely spoken by anyone under 60. Despite his family calling the effort impractical, hundreds have signed up to learn the dialect their parents spoke, calling it a dream come true.
stories.extended_summary: Dan Sheena puts on a blond wig and plays an Iraqi couple bickering in Judeo-Arabic — a language his own family told him was dying and not worth saving. The 31-year-old Israeli grew up speaking it at home with his Baghdad-born parents, a rarity among younger Iraqi Jews whose families stopped passing it down to assimilate. When he launched TikTok videos and Zoom lessons in 2023, the response overwhelmed him. People registered in droves, saying they'd heard their parents speak Judeo-Arabic and had always dreamed of learning it themselves. Now Sheena's garage classroom is helping second- and third-generation Jews reclaim a language nearly lost to history.

stories.title: Living Water Connections Turns Comics Into Literacy Tool for Kids
stories.summary: After Adin Behrens got stuck in a hospital with Crohn's disease, he created a comic newspaper made by famous artists to give hospitalized kids something exciting to read. His father turned that idea into Living Water Connections, a nonprofit now delivering comics and graphic novels to young patients while building literacy skills.
stories.extended_summary: Adin Behrens was stuck in a hospital bed with Crohn's disease, bored and feeling like there was nothing for him there. So he imagined something different: a comic newspaper drawn by famous artists that could make hospital stays less miserable for kids like him. His father Justin turned that idea into Living Water Connections, a State College-based nonprofit that now delivers comics, graphic novels, and a quarterly newspaper to hospitalized children. The initiative has grown beyond hospitals into a full literacy program, using unconventional reading material to help young readers build skills while giving them something positive during their hardest days.

stories.title: From hay field to full-diet farm: Rainshadow Organics grows a community in Central Oregon
stories.summary: Sarahlee Lawrence turned her family's 80-acre hay field into Rainshadow Organics, an ambitious full-diet farm that grows nearly everything 70 families eat — vegetables, grains, dairy, eggs, and meat. The shift started after an essay changed how she thought about food's environmental impact.
stories.extended_summary: Sarahlee Lawrence spent years as a Grand Canyon river guide before Michael Pollan's writing made her realize food choices matter more for the environment than she'd thought. She came home to her family's Central Oregon hay operation and asked if she could try something radical. Now their 80 acres produce vegetables, grains, dairy, eggs, and meat for roughly 70 families through a "full-diet" CSA program — an experiment in local food that aims to feed people almost everything they eat. Another 180 families participate in seasonal vegetable boxes. What began as one woman's environmental awakening has become a community-supported model for rethinking how farms feed neighborhoods.

stories.title: UADA’s Taunya Ernst recognized for contributions to high tunnel production in Arkansas
stories.summary: University of Arkansas educator Taunya Ernst received the NRCS State Conservationist's Partnership Award for her work promoting high tunnel farming in Arkansas. She authored the state's first production guide after noticing abandoned structures across the state where frustrated growers had given up without proper regional guidance.
stories.extended_summary: Taunya Ernst kept seeing the same thing across Arkansas: high tunnel structures sitting abandoned, left behind by growers who'd gotten excited, put them up, then hit a wall of frustration. The temporary structures extend growing seasons and protect crops, but first-time users lacked region-specific guidance on making them work economically. Ernst joined the University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture in 2023 and wrote the state's first high tunnel production guide, tailored specifically for Arkansas conditions. For helping growers actually succeed instead of giving up, the USDA's Natural Resources Conservation Service awarded her their State Conservationist's Partnership Award this month.

She sat beside donation boxes to engage students directly, making the experience interactive instead of letting boxes sit empty around campus.
stories.extended_summary: Blaire Jones's freshman mentees at Howard University kept asking the same question over winter break: what kind of professional clothes should they get for the semester ahead? The biology junior realized most freshmen arrive without the suits and formal wear they'll need for Howard's professional culture. She'd seen donation boxes scattered across campus before, but they usually sat empty because students walked past without engaging. So Jones did something different for her MLK Day weekend clothing drive: she sat next to the box herself, making it interactive and explaining the mission face-to-face. Students stopped, donated, and connected — proving sometimes the difference between success and an empty box is just showing up in person.

stories.title: Udaipur Man Builds Stove That Cooks For 25 People In 30 Minutes
stories.summary: Sher Khan, a Class 8 dropout from Udaipur, built the Vishwaguru Chulha 27 years ago after watching women spend hours cooking in hot kitchens. His patented stove cooks food for 25 people in 30 minutes, uses just 2 kg of wood instead of 10, and can boil dal, bake rotis, and make pizza simultaneously.
stories.extended_summary: Sher Khan watched women in Udaipur spend hours sweating over separate fires to cook baatis and dal, and decided there had to be a better way. The Class 8 dropout and iron craftsman started tinkering 27 years ago. What emerged was the Vishwaguru Chulha — a multi-level stove that boils, fries, and bakes all at once. Dal cooks below while rotis bake on top and pizza grills on the side. It feeds 25 people in 30 minutes using just 2 kg of wood instead of the usual 10, producing far less smoke. Khan secured a patent in 2017, and now dhabas and small businesses across India are discovering that sometimes the most practical innovations come from people who never finished school.

stories.title: How to Build Cities Out of Plants and Earth
stories.summary: Hempbuild's Ronan McDermott is bringing hemp construction blocks to Ireland's notoriously damp climate. The blocks regulate indoor humidity, prevent mold, and are easier to lay than concrete, and they're already gaining traction across Europe in apartment complexes and commercial buildings as a carbon-storing alternative to traditional materials.
stories.extended_summary: Ireland's winter rains are brutal, and dampness is the biggest problem builders face. Ronan McDermott thinks chunky blocks made mostly from hemp are the answer. His company Hempbuild supplies construction blocks that regulate indoor humidity and prevent mold — crucial in a country where wet is the default setting. They're easy to lay like regular bricks and strong insulators, though they can't bear much weight. While still uncommon in Ireland, hemp blocks are catching on across Europe, going into apartment complexes and large commercial projects. The blocks store carbon absorbed by hemp plants during growth, making them a climate-friendlier building material than concrete or traditional options.
stories.headline_split: How to Build Cities Out of

stories.title: The cargo ship that’s powered the old-fashioned way
stories.summary: Neoline's cargo ship Neoliner Origin uses giant sails to provide up to 70% of its propulsion, making it feel like a "flying carpet" according to CEO Jean Zanuttini. The ship aims to emit only a fifth as much carbon pollution as traditional cargo vessels while carrying over 5,000 tons on a regular route between Baltimore and France.
stories.extended_summary: Most cargo ships burn fossil fuels and pump out carbon pollution. The Neoliner Origin looks like it sailed out of history — because in a way, it did. The ship has giant sails that provide up to 70% of its propulsion, and CEO Jean Zanuttini says when they're up, it's silent, magical, like a flying carpet gliding across the water. Combined with hybrid diesel-electric engines and other efficiencies, Neoline aims to emit only a fifth of a traditional cargo ship's carbon footprint while still carrying over 5,000 tons. The company launched a regular Baltimore-to-France route last fall, betting they can prove ancient technology makes for cost-effective, climate-friendly modern shipping.

stories.title: How St. Jude Raised $4.5 Billion in Bequests in a Decade
stories.summary: St. Jude Children's Research Hospital raised $4.5 billion in bequests over a decade by building deep relationships with donors like Hubert Morales, a retired bricklayer whose family donated for generations. The hospital combines direct mail, digital outreach, and personal connections to create a planned-giving powerhouse.
stories.extended_summary: Hubert Morales and his wife Julie always planned to leave St. Jude Children's Research Hospital in their will. Julie's family in Peoria had donated every year, watched the telethon, knew pediatric patients personally. When Julie died of cancer in 2018, Hubert kept the commitment. He's one of thousands of legacy donors who've pledged bequests to St. Jude, which raised $4.5 billion in planned gifts over the past decade. The hospital's fundraising arm says the secret is trust, technology, and high-touch relationships — meeting donors where they are, whether through direct mail or digital channels, and building connections that last generations.

stories.title: Citizen of the Week: The Flower Girl
stories.summary: Leona Davis started Forget Me Knot in 2016 to repurpose wedding flowers into smaller bouquets for hospice patients and nursing home residents. What began with 25 hydrangea arrangements in her garage has grown into a Philadelphia operation that's delivered thousands of bouquets, with patients now greeting her as "the flower girl."
stories.extended_summary: Leona Davis stood in her garage staring at 25 massive hydrangea arrangements from an Academy of Music wedding, wondering what they'd do with all those flowers. Her daughter's wedding was coming up, and the question lingered: where do these gorgeous arrangements go after one night? Davis turned that problem into Forget Me Knot, a business built on sustainability and smiles. She repurposes wedding flowers into smaller bouquets for patients in hospice and nursing homes across Philadelphia and New Jersey. Nearly a decade later, she's delivered thousands of arrangements, and patients light up when they see her. "Here comes the flower girl," they say now, grateful for the fresh blooms that would have otherwise been thrown away.

What started 18 years ago as a cart selling pens has grown into what staff call 'the Macy's of the East End,' preparing students for life beyond the classroom through hands-on vocational training.
stories.extended_summary: Dillan Wysocki, 16, counts change and rings up snacks at his school's student-run store — and he's getting really good at it. He's one of more than a dozen special education students at Mattituck High School who spend a period each day working in a store that's become a beloved campus hub for snacks and supplies. Teacher Caroline Wills launched the program 18 years ago when she felt traditional special education wasn't practical enough. stories.title: Rotary extending its lifesaving rescue station program up and down coastline
stories.summary: Sunrise Rotary Vero Beach has installed 64 bright orange rescue stations at Florida beaches, with plans to expand from Sebastian Inlet to Fort Pierce Inlet. The lifesaving flotation devices, funded by sponsorships and placed at no cost to communities, offer a simple solution for rip current emergencies: call 911, throw the buoy, help someone stay afloat.
stories.extended_summary: Bright orange life preservers in wooden frames have been sprouting along Florida's barrier island beaches — 64 so far, with more coming. Rotary Rescue Stations are the work of Sunrise Rotary Vero Beach, which has raised $20,000 and secured materials from Home Depot to install these flotation devices at public beaches, resorts, and HOA communities from Sebastian Inlet to Fort Pierce Inlet. If someone's caught in a rip current, beachgoers call 911, throw the rescue buoy, and tell the struggling swimmer to hold on until help arrives. Justin Lefebure, the Rotarian leading the project, puts it simply: 'If it saves just one life, it will be worth it to us.'

stories.title: 'Health by stealth': doors always open for men at the Shed
stories.summary: Men's Shed chapters across Grey-Bruce, Ontario offer retired men somewhere to go, something to do, and someone to talk to. Part of an international movement combating social isolation, these groups bring men together in casual settings — like the cozy legion hall in Ferndale — where they can connect, work on projects, and build friendships they didn't realize they needed.
stories.extended_summary: Gerry Tilmans didn't think of himself as particularly social, but when his wife nudged him to check out Men's Shed, he found what he didn't know he was missing. Now, men with grey hair gather Saturday mornings around long tables in a wood-paneled legion hall north of Ferndale, drinking coffee and sharing sandwiches after their monthly meeting. It's part of an international movement in 15 countries with over 140 Canadian chapters, all built on a simple premise: men, especially retirees, need connection. Many arrive stuck in their garages, lacking local friends. Men's Shed gives them a place to belong.

stories.title: Saving the shells: A project to rebuild Alabama’s oyster reefs
stories.summary: Alabama Extension is turning discarded oyster shells into ecosystem rebuilding tools, keeping them out of landfills and back in coastal waters. Oyster reefs filter water, provide marine habitat, and stabilize shorelines — and researchers are working with communities to restore these critical structures that support both Gulf Coast ecosystems and local seafood economies.
stories.extended_summary: 'Oyster shells don't belong in a landfill,' says P.J. Waters, a coastal fisheries specialist with Alabama Extension. Instead, they're ecosystem engineers — building reef structures that house fish, filter nutrients from the water, and protect shorelines. Every oyster filters phytoplankton and sends heavier particles sinking to the sea floor, improving water quality with each cycle. Despite their importance, Alabama's reefs have declined dramatically. Now, through research and community engagement, Extension researchers are reclaiming shells from restaurants and returning them to the water, rebuilding the foundation of Alabama's estuaries one shell at a time.

stories.title: Ex-Army Man's Family Dug 70 Trenches, Planted 450+ Trees & Revived 8 Lakh Litres of Water in Junnar
stories.summary: Ex-Army man Ramesh Kharmale and his family spend weekends transforming Junnar's barren hills, digging 70 contour trenches and planting 450+ native trees to capture 800,000 liters of rainwater. After 17 years in the military, the 49-year-old forest guard found his true mission: healing land ravaged by climate change, one spade at a time.
stories.extended_summary: Ramesh Kharmale spent 300 grueling hours carving 70 trenches into a mountainside — timing the project to his birthday as a gift to the earth. The 49-year-old ex-serviceman turned forest guard now climbs Junnar's sun-baked hills every weekend with his wife Swati and their children, armed with spades and pickaxes. While Ramesh digs water-trapping trenches, Swati clears invasive weeds from ancient stepwells, and their kids scatter native seeds into freshly dug pits. Together, they've planted over 450 trees and captured 800,000 liters of rainwater on land once left barren. 'Conservation is my passion, but it's also my duty,' says Kharmale, who redirected his military discipline toward a new battle: saving India's hills.

stories.title: The Digital Divide After Prison: How Cornell Is Reimagining Reentry Through Technology
stories.summary: Cornell's Criminal Justice and Employment Initiative is tackling a hidden barrier to reentry: digital literacy. When a man released after 20 years asked 'What is email?', researchers created Rézme, a platform helping formerly incarcerated job seekers navigate automated hiring systems designed to filter them out before a human ever sees their application.
stories.extended_summary: The man had been out of prison for exactly one day when he asked Jodi Anderson Jr. a question that stopped him cold: 'What is email?' After 20 years inside, the man had never used a smartphone, sent an email, or touched modern technology — yet was expected to apply for jobs, housing, and benefits entirely online. That moment drove Anderson, director of technological innovation at Cornell's School of Industrial and Labor Relations, to co-found Rézme, a platform designed to help people navigate automated hiring algorithms that reject applications before they reach human eyes. 'Technology created many of these barriers,' Anderson explains. 'So technology has to be part of the solution.' It's a recognition that digital access isn't just convenience — it's survival.
stories.headline_split: The Digital Divide After Prison: How Cornell Is Reimagining Reentry

stories.title: Church’s pet food pantry celebrates four years in business
stories.summary: Valley Community Pet Food Pantry has distributed over 31,000 pounds of food in four years to pet owners who need help caring for their animals. Operating from a church in Arenas Valley, volunteers provide food for everything from cats and dogs to goldfish, ferrets, and even chickens — because for many people, a pet is their only family.
stories.extended_summary: Alma Faye Wilson noticed something missing in her community: while food pantries existed for people, nobody was regularly helping with pet food. So in February 2022, Valley Community Pet Food Pantry opened at Valley Community Church in Arenas Valley, New Mexico. Four years later, they've given away more than 31,000 pounds of food — not just for dogs and cats, but for goldfish, gerbils, hamsters, ferrets, pet rats, and a couple of special chickens (one trained to walk on a leash). Open the first and third Saturdays each month, the pantry operates on a simple understanding: 'People love their pets, and want to provide for them. In some cases, a dog or cat is a person's only local family,' Wilson says.

In a tupik beside the centre's shed, 23-year-old Nicholas Flowers prepares sealskin for kamek boots, using his grandmother's ulu — passing knowledge forward.
stories.extended_summary: Nicholas Flowers sits in a tupik on spruce boughs and snow, using his grandmother's ulu to clean a sealskin that will become kamek boots. The 23-year-old is director of community outreach for the Inotsiavik Centre, a youth-led nonprofit that won the $1 million Arctic Inspiration Prize in 2024. He and his co-founders were 'flabbergasted' — they'd applied with good hopes but never expected to win. Now the money is funding in-person and online programs in Nunatsiavut, teaching Inuttitut language and traditional skills. A kettle boils for tea on the woodstove beside Flowers as he works, connecting young Inuit with cultural practices that ground them in who they are.

Students ages 8-18 learn to turn ideas into businesses, build brands, and pitch to community investors — all guided by the philosophy: 'If we reach them now, we do not have to rescue them later.'
stories.extended_summary: Torrus McGill, an award-winning educator in Tuscaloosa, founded Empowering Leaders of Tomorrow on one core belief: reach young people early with real opportunities, and you won't have to rescue them from negative outcomes later. ELOT's Youth Business Institute offers students ages 8-18 a free nine-week program where they learn to turn ideas into businesses, understand financial literacy, and work with local entrepreneurs. The program culminates in Youth Business Pitch Night, where students present their ventures to community leaders and potential investors. It's about more than business skills — it's about creating pathways before kids need rescuing.

stories.title: Teachers Move Beyond AI Basics to More Sophisticated Instructional Uses
stories.summary: The National Academy for AI Instruction is training 400,000 teachers to use AI for sophisticated instructional support, not just lesson templates. At a recent New York City session, 50 teachers learned to build 'agentic' AI tools that can stress-test lessons for gaps, refine teaching approaches, and support deeper learning — moving beyond basic automation to real pedagogical partnership.
stories.extended_summary: Jing Liang Guan doesn't just want AI to write a lesson plan — he wants it to challenge his thinking, spot content gaps, and help him become a better teacher over time. The Brooklyn Science and Engineering Academy teacher was among 50 educators at a March 18 training in New York City learning to develop 'agentic' AI: autonomous systems that handle complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. It's part of a five-year, $23 million partnership between the American Federation of Teachers and three major AI developers to train 400,000 teachers in sophisticated AI use. As AI adoption among teachers nearly doubled from 2024 to 2025, the focus is shifting from surface-level tasks to tools that genuinely support instruction — leveraging teachers' professional judgment rather than replacing it.
